text: The surgeon uses a self-retaining retractor to expose the full thickness prolapse segment. Cautery is applied one centimeter proximal to the dentate line to circumscribe the rectal wall, completing the circumferential rectotomy. Upon entering the anterior peritoneum, the sigmoid colon is delivered transanally, and its mesentery is divided using a vessel sealing device, modifying the standard Altemeier operation approach.

The surgeon uses a GIA stapler to divide the sigmoid colon, then tags the staple line with a suture for later retrieval. A Babcock forceps is used to reposition the sigmoid colon into the abdominopelvic cavity. A TAMUS port access channel is secured to the dermis at the one and seven o'clock positions, and the port lid is clamped onto the channel, initiating insufflation. The surgeon divides adhesions and explores the abdomen, noting the omentum adherent to the anterior abdominal wall. For the rectopexy, the sigmoid conduit is moved to the patient's left, exposing the sacral promontory. The staple line is distracted caudally, and a laparoscopic tacker secures the sigmoid epiploics to the sacral promontory with three absorbable Vicryl tacks.

Endocyanine green is administered to assess bowel wall perfusion before removing the TAMIS port. The tagged sigmoid colon is delivered towards the anorectum, and the staple line is excised. A hand-sewn coloanal anastomosis is performed using interrupted 2-0 vicryl sutures. Endocyanine green is used again to confirm conduit perfusion, and the completed anastomosis is displayed.

The surgeon uses synthetic mesh to reinforce the repair and PEXI. After performing a rectotomy and dividing the mesentery, the sigmoid conduit is stapled and tagged with a suture. The tamus port is positioned, and pneumopelvis is established with the patient in Trendelenburg position. The sigmoid conduit is delivered back into the abdominal cavity, exposing the posterior pelvis. A six-centimeter section of polypropylene mesh is delivered via the tamus port and tacked into place with absorbable tacks. The tamis port is removed, and the tagged staple line is delivered to the operative field. The distal portion of the mesh is divided to create two pant legs, which are wrapped around the sigmoid colon conduit and secured at the three and nine o'clock positions with three O PDS suture. The anastomosis is completed using a hand-sewn technique.
